coredata.dc:title: pH-Sensitive blue and red N-CDs for L-asparaginase quantification in complex biological matrices
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Alanazi, Ahmed Z.
Alhazzani, Khalid
Ibrahim, Hossieny
Mostafa, Aya M.
Barker, James
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Bellah H. Ali, Al-Montaser
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorometric method for the determination of L-asparaginase, an enzyme crucial in cancer therapy and food industry applications, is presented. This sensitive and selective approach utilizes L-asparagine and two pH-sensitive carbon dots (blue-N-CDs and red-N-CDs) as probes. The interaction between L-asparagine and L-asparaginase liberates ammonia, causing an increase in pH. This pH change simultaneously decreases the fluorescence of blue-N-CDs while enhancing the emission of red-N-CDs, enabling ratiometric detection of L-asparaginase. Comprehensive characterization of both carbon dots and investigation of their response mechanism towards L-asparaginase were conducted using ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry, fluorescence spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging techniques. The designed approach demonstrates outstanding linearity (20 to 2000 U L-1) and a low detection limit (6.95 U L-1) for L-asparaginase quantification. Moreover, when tested to human serum samples, the detection system exhibits outstanding selectivity and high recovery rates (96.15% to 99.75%) with low standard deviation, underscoring its suitability for practical implementation in clinical diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: L-asparaginase
Fluorometric
pH-sensitive
Carbon dots
Ratiometric

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) blue-N-CDs and (B) red-N-CDs. The insets of figures are the average diameters (Gaussian fitting) of both CDs, XRD pattern of (C) blue-N-CDs and (D) red-N-CDs, FTIR spectrum of (E) blue-N-CDs and (F) red-N-CDs.

(A) Ultraviolet visible absorption (red line), fluorescence excitation (blue line) and photoluminescence (black line) spectra of the blue-N-CDs. (B) Emission spectra of blue-N-CDs (20 μg mL−1) upon scanning different excitation wavelengths (start from 330 nm to 440 nm with a 10 nm increment), (C) Ultraviolet visible absorption (red line), fluorescence excitation (blue line) and photoluminescence (black line) spectra of the red-N-CDs, (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of red-N-CDs (20 μg mL−1): excitation wavelengths starts from 310 to 430 nm.

(A) Photoluminsence spectra of the (blue-N-CDs/red-N-CDs/L-asparagine) system with different L-asparaginase concentrations (20 to 2000 U L-1). (B) A linear relationship between F645/F445 and L-asparaginase concentrations (20 to 2000 U L-1).
Response of the (blue-N-CDs/red-N-CDs/ L-Asparagine) system to different (A) amino acids, (B) metal ions and anions.

TEM images of blue-N-CDs (A) without L-asparaginase and (B) with L-asparaginase. (C) Zeta (ζ) potential of aqueous blue-N-CDs solution at various L-asparaginase concentrations. TEM images of red-N-CDs (D) without L-asparaginase and (E) with L-asparaginase. (F) Zeta (ζ) potential of aqueous red-N-CDs solution at various L-asparaginase concentrations.
Comparison of the reported methods for L-asparaginase detection with proposed method.
Determination of L-asparaginase in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: pH-Sensitive blue and red N-CDs for L-asparaginase quantification in complex biological matrices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorometric method for the determination of L-asparaginase, an enzyme crucial in cancer therapy and food industry applications, is presented. This sensitive and selective approach utilizes L-asparagine and two pH-sensitive carbon dots (blue-N-CDs and red-N-CDs) as probes. The interaction between L-asparagine and L-asparaginase liberates ammonia, causing an increase in pH. This pH change simultaneously decreases the fluorescence of blue-N-CDs while enhancing the emission of red-N-CDs, enabling ratiometric detection of L-asparaginase. Comprehensive characterization of both carbon dots and investigation of their response mechanism towards L-asparaginase were conducted using ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry, fluorescence spectroscopy, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging techniques. The designed approach demonstrates outstanding linearity (20 to 2000 U L-1) and a low detection limit (6.95 U L-1) for L-asparaginase quantification. Moreover, when tested to human serum samples, the detection system exhibits outstanding selectivity and high recovery rates (96.15% to 99.75%) with low standard deviation, underscoring its suitability for practical implementation in clinical diagnostics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: L-asparaginase is a crucial enzyme in chemotherapy, primarily utilized for treating acute lymphoblastic leukemia in children [1]. L-asparaginase plays a crucial role in cancer therapy by exploiting a metabolic vulnerability of certain leukemic cells. These malignant cells heavily depend on extracellular asparagine for their rapid growth and proliferation. The enzyme functions by hydrolyzing asparagine into aspartic acid and ammonia, effectively depleting the available asparagine in the bloodstream. This depletion creates a nutrient-deficient environment for the leukemic cells, which, unlike normal cells, often lack the ability to synthesize asparagine internally. Consequently, this enzymatic action impairs protein synthesis in cancer cells, hindering their growth and ultimately leading to cell death [2]. Many leukemic cells differ from normal cells in their incapacity to generate L-asparagine, primarily attributed to the absence of asparagine synthetase, making L-asparaginase particularly effective [3]. Humans do not naturally produce L-asparaginase, so it is sourced from bacteria such as Escherichia coli and Erwinia chrysanthemi, with the former often preferred due to its higher efficiency and longer plasma half-life, though the latter is used when there is hypersensitivity to derivatives from E. coli [4,5]. However, the use of L-asparaginase can lead to significant side effects, including allergic reactions, pancreatitis, coagulation abnormalities, and neurotoxicity. Hypersensitivity reactions are particularly concerning and can range from mild to severe anaphylaxis [6]. These reactions are believed to stem from the foreign protein nature of the bacterial enzymes, prompting an immune response in some patients. Consequently, dose adjustment and close monitoring are critical in the administration of L-asparaginase to mitigate these risks and tailor treatment to individual patient tolerance levels. Enzymatic activity of L-Asparaginase is assessed by measuring the conversion of L-Asparagine substrate at its maximum rate. In this context, One unit of L-Asparaginase (U) is described as the quantity of enzyme required to convert 1 μmol of L-Asparagine into 1 μmol of L-Aspartic acid and 1 μmol of NH3 within a minute at a temperature of 37 °C [7]. Various quantification methods have been developed to determine the products resulting from the conversion process.

These approaches include colorimetric assays [7–9], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11], electrophoresis [12], direct amino acid quantification using circular dichroism [13], and conductometry [14]. The most common method for quantifying L-asparaginase activity is the colorimetric Nessler method, which measures ammonia released from the transition of L-asparagine to L-aspartic acid by reacting it with Nessler’s reagent to produce a yellow color detectable by spectrophotometry [8]. Although this strategy offers high reproducibility, it requires careful handling due to its use of toxic reagents, has a relatively high limit of detection, and is prone to interference from various contaminants in complex media. To address these limitations, alternative methods such as the L-aspartic acid ß-hydroxamate (AHA) method and the indooxine method have been developed [7]. The AHA method involves converting L-asparagine to L-aspartic acid β-hydroxamate, which reacts with ferric chloride to produce a red color, while the indooxine method creates a green dye by reacting hydroxylamine (liberated from AHA hydrolysis) with 8-hydroxyquinoline [15]. The indooxine method is particularly sensitive but is limited by the pH stability of hydroxylamine [16]. Another approach for measuring L-asparaginase activity utilizes a linked enzyme system to monitor NADH oxidation kinetics. This spectrophotometric technique employs an excess of glutamic-oxaloacetate transaminase and malic dehydrogenase as auxiliary enzymes. The rate of NADH consumption in this multi-step reaction cascade serves as an indirect indicator of L-asparaginase activity [17]. HPLC-based methods provide a more reliable alternative, overcoming the drawbacks of colorimetric assays by quantifying L-aspartic acid directly or through the depletion of L-asparagine [10]. These methods feature high reproducibility, accuracy, and precision, and involve the derivatization of products with o-phthaldialdehyde for detection by fluorescence [11]. The significant disadvantage of using HPLC methods for determining asparaginase activity is their complex setup and operation, requiring extensive sample preparation and specialized equipment, which can be time-consuming and costly. Alternative approaches for measuring the liberated ammonia involve a kinetic enzymatic approach that utilizes an ammonia gas-sensing electrode [18]. However, a limitation of gas-sensing method is its vulnerability to interference from other volatile substances in the sample, which can potentially result in inaccurate measurements.

The fluorescence analysis methods are highly sensitive, selective, and user-friendly, making it appropriate for both qualitative and quantitative assessments of diverse samples. Moreover, its robust and straightforward design is particularly well-suited for high-throughput detection of biological samples. To date, to our knowledge, two fluorogenic probes for L-asparaginase have been reported. The first fluorometric method for detecting L-asparaginase utilizes L-aspartic acid b-(7-amido-4-methylcoumarin) as a substrate, yet comprehensive demonstrations of its kinetics, selectivity, and efficiency of detection in complex biological samples have not been provided [19]. On the other hand, the second probe, relying on the fluorescent dye 6-naphthylamine-2,3-dicarboxylate (TPAN). The TPAN-Asn method may produce inconsistent results when detecting L-asparaginase due to its vulnerability to environmental fluctuations, such as changes in temperature and pH [20]. Additionally, the presence of other biomolecules in complex biological samples like serum can interfere with the probe, impacting both the accuracy and the reliability of the fluorescence response.

Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent nanomaterials that have gained significant attention as probes due to their excellent properties, including biocompatibility, photostability, and tunable optical characteristics [21,22]. These carbon-based nanoparticles, typically less than 10 nm in size, can be synthesized using a variety of techniques, such as hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and electrochemical methods, making them relatively easy and cost-effective to produce [23,24]. Compared to traditional synthesized fluorescent probes, carbon dots offer advantages like lower toxicity, improved photostability, and versatile functionalization, expanding their potential applications in fields like bioimaging, sensing, and optoelectronics [25,26].

This work presents an innovative approach to evaluate L-asparaginase activity by utilizing the unique fluorescence properties of two distinct CDs. Upon the addition of L-asparagine, the substrate for L-asparaginase, the enzyme catalyzes its conversion, liberating ammonia as a by-product. The novelty lies in the differential response of the blue-emitting and red-emitting nitrogen doped CDs (Blue-N-CDs and Red-N-CDs) to the change in pH caused by the released ammonia. While the fluorescence of the blue-N-CDs declines, the red-N-CDs exhibit an increase in fluorescence intensity. This contrasting behavior forms the basis for a ratiometric sensing strategy, enabling precise quantification of L-asparaginase activity. Significantly, this innovative strategy has been successfully employed to human serum samples, demonstrating its practical utility and potential for clinical applications. The work leverages the unique properties of CDs and their pH-sensitive fluorescence to design a sensitive, selective, and robust analytical method for L-asparaginase determination.

L-Asparaginase (lyophilized powder, 100 units/mg of protein) isolated from E. coli was obtained from Merck, Darmstadt, Germany. L-asparagine, p-phenylenediamine, ethanol, L-serine, L-tryptophan, and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, FeCl3, CoCl2, NiCl2, CuCl2, NaF, NaI, NaCO3, NaHCO3, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, Na2S, Na2HPO4, glutathione, urea, ascorbic acid, arginine, cysteine, L-proline, lysine, L-aspartic acid, leucine, glycine, glutamine, histidine, methionine, threonine, tyrosine, ornithine, phenylalanine, and valine were purchased El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Co., Cairo, Egypt. Double distilled water (DDW) obtained from Aquatron Automatic Water Still apparatus (Model 4L/Hr) was used in all aqueous solutions.
Detailed information regarding the instrumentation used for the experiment can be found in the Supplementary Data file.

Red-N-CDs were synthesized using a slightly modified previously reported one-step microwave-assisted method, starting from p-phenylenediamine [27]. Initially, 0.054 g of p-phenylenediamine was suspended in 50 mL of a 1:1 water/ethanol mixture. This solution was subjected to sonication for 15 min to achieve uniformity before being subjected to microwave heating at 900 W (Jack, China) for one hour. During the heating, an ethanol/water mixture in a 1:1 ratio was continuously introduced into the solution. Following temperature equilibration to ambient conditions, the solution underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for a duration of 10 min. This process facilitated the separation and removal of larger particulate matter. Subsequently, the CDs underwent further purification through a 1000 kDa bag filter. Blue-N-CDs were synthesized employing slightly modified procedures of a previously reported hydrothermal method [28]. Blue-N-CDs preparation commenced by dissolving precise quantities of L-serine (0.157g) and L-tryptophan (0.1022g) in 10 mL of mildly alkaline water, where the pH was adjusted to approximately 8.1 using 0.1 M NaOH. This precursor solution was then moved to a 30 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at an elevated temperature of 200 °C extending for 8 h. Upon completion of the thermal treatment, the autoclave was left to gradually return to ambient temperature through passive cooling. The as-synthesized CDs were further purified by employing a 1000 kDa bag filter to remove any impurities or unreacted precursors. The purified carbon dots were subsequently lyophilized and the resulting powder was stored at 4 °C. To achieve the desired concentration, an appropriate amount of the lyophilized powder was dissolved in DDW.
The procedures and calculations required to determine the quantum yield are provided in detail in the Supplementary Data file.

The quantification of L-asparaginase activity was conducted using a modified approach based on the method proposed by Mashburn and Wriston [12]. The assay involved combining 0.1 mL of the purified enzyme solution with 1.0 mL of 0.04 M L-asparagine solution and 0.9 mL of 1.0 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). The mixture was left to incubate at 37 °C for 10 min to facilitate the enzymatic reaction. Subsequently, the reaction was terminated by rapidly cooling the mixture in an ice bath and immediately adding 1.0 mL of ice-cooled 99 % (v/v) ethanol. The reaction contents were then subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. After centrifugation, the upper layer was retrieved, and 1.0 mL of this layer was diluted with double distilled water to a final volume of 8.0 mL. To this diluted mixture, 1.0 mL of red-N-CDs (5.0 μg mL−1) and 1.0 mL of blue-N-CDs (25.0 μg mL−1) were introduced. Following the initiation of the reaction, a 1.0-minute incubation period was observed to allow for sufficient progression of the enzymatic process. Subsequently, fluorescence emission measurements were conducted using an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

Patient serum samples were retrieved from Assiut University Hospital for this investigation. Three distinct samples were collected and promptly frozen to preserve their integrity. Prior to analysis, the samples were subjected to centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting supernatant was carefully extracted using a syringe and subsequently passed through a 0.45 μm microporous filter to ensure purity. This filtered serum was then diluted to a 1 % concentration in preparation for mixing with various L-asparaginase solutions. To prepare the L-asparaginase solutions, a stock was created by dissolving asparaginase-Medac® (10,000 U) in 5 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (pH 7.5), resulting in a concentration of 2,000 U mL−1. This stock solution was then used to create a series of dilutions in drug-free blank serum, yielding final concentrations spanning from 20 to 2000 U L-1.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images reveal distinct characteristics of the blue-N-CDs and red-N-CDs. The blue-N-CDs are predominantly spherical with minor variations in particle size, as depicted in Fig. 1 A. The inset of Fig. 1A indicates a mean particle size of 4.2 nm. In contrast, the red-N-CDs exhibit uniformly spherical and monodispersed particles, as shown in Fig. 1B, with a mean particle size of 2.4 nm, highlighted in the inset of Fig. 1B. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of blue-N-CDs and red-N-CDs show broad peaks at 19.1° and 24.5° respectively, indicative of their amorphous or disordered graphite structures (Fig. 1C and D). The peak at 24.5° in blue-N-CDs, closer to typical graphitic structures, suggests a higher degree of graphitization, contributing to blue photoluminescence through efficient π-π* transitions [29]. Conversely, the red-N-CDs’ peak at 19.1° indicates a less graphitic nature, leading to red emission likely due to more defects and edge sites that trap excitons, influencing their emission properties [30]. Blue-N-CDs' FTIR spectrum demonstrates several distinct peaks (Fig. 1E). Broad peaks at 3216 and 3070 cm−1 are attributed to NH stretching from amine groups and OH stretching from hydroxyl groups, indicating strong hydrogen bonding [31,32]. The shoulder band at 2800 cm−1 is a probable indication of CH stretching from aliphatic chains. A very intense peak at 1712 cm−1 signifies CO stretching oscillations, typical of carbonyl groups from carboxylic acids or amides, while the band at 1397 cm−1 suggests symmetric bending of carboxylate ions. Smaller peaks at 1178 cm−1 and 1061 cm−1 are consistent with C-N stretching and C-O stretching oscillations, respectively. Additional small peaks at 762, 645, and 529 cm−1 are related to bending oscillations in aromatic or heterocyclic structures, confirming the presence of tryptophan's indole ring. The FTIR spectrum of red-N-CDs exhibits several characteristic peaks indicating the presence of various functional groups (Fig. 1F). The most intense band at 3427 cm−1 is typically indicative of NH stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of primary amines or amide groups. The weak peak at 2916 cm−1 can be related to the C–H stretching oscillations from methyl or methylene groups, which could be part of the carbon dot structure or surface modifications [25]. The peak at 1631 cm−1 is linked to carbonyl group stretching of carbonyl groups, commonly seen in amides or carboxyl groups, suggesting oxidation during the synthesis process. The low intensity peak at 1376 cm−1 can be related to C-N stretching oscillations of aromatic amines, which is consistent with the nitrogen-doping from the original p-phenylenediamine. Finally, the peak at 1018 cm−1 is generally ascribed to C-O stretching, indicative of ether or alcohol groups [33]. The XPS spectra of both blue-N-CDs and red-N-CDs reveal three characteristic peaks, related to the binding energies of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. For blue-N-CDs, these peaks appear at 288.19, 398.54, and 533.15 eV, respectively (Fig. S1A), while for red-N-CDs, they are observed at 289.83, 400.18, and 534.79 eV (Fig. S1B). The presence of these peaks confirms the incorporation of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen atoms in both types of carbon dots.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the blue-N-CDs displays three distinct peaks at 235 nm, 270 nm, and 350 nm (Fig. 2 A). The absorption spectrum exhibited a distinct peak at 235 nm, which can be attributed to π-π* electronic transitions occurring within the aromatic sp2 domains of the carbon dot structure. The signal at 270 nm is likely associated with the n-π* transitions of the functional groups containing lone pairs. The peak at 350 nm can be indicative of the presence of conjugated system. The fluorescence excitation spectrum reveals two prominent peaks at 295 nm and 350 nm. The peak at 350 nm exhibits higher intensity compared to the peak at 295 nm. Consequently, the excitation wavelength of 350 nm was our selection for subsequent fluorescence measurements. The fluorescence emission spectrum was monitored after excitation from 330 nm to 440 nm (Fig. 2B). The optimal emission wavelength was observed at 445 nm, corresponding to excitation at 350 nm. It is worth noting that excitation wavelengths below 330 nm were not considered due to the distorted peak shape obtained in that region. Interestingly, a shift in the emission wavelength was observed as the excitation wavelength was varied. This phenomenon, known as excitation-dependent emission, can be ascribed to the non-uniformity in particle size, as indicated by the TEM images (Fig. 1A and B). The red-N-CDs exhibit specific spectroscopic properties in their ultraviolet visible and photoluminescence spectra. The ultraviolet visible spectrum displays two broad and relatively weak absorption peaks at 270 nm and 345 nm, which might correspond to π-π* transitions and n-π* transitions (Fig. 2C). The photoluminescence analysis reveals that the optimal emission wavelength at 649 nm after excitation at 360 nm. This excitation wavelength was determined after scanning from 310 nm to 430 nm, where 360 nm was found to be the most effective for eliciting strong emission (Fig. 2D). Red-N-CDs demonstrate excitation-independent emission behavior. The quantum yield of red-N-CDs is measured at 12 %, which is lower compared to 23 % for the blue-N-CDs.

The stability of the as-prepared carbon dots (blue-N-CDs and red-N-CDs) was systematically investigated under various environmental conditions, including changes in pH, ionic strength, and UV irradiation exposure. The fluorescence intensity of blue-N-CDs demonstrated a decrease as the pH increased from 2 to 12 (Fig. S2A). Conversely, the photoluminescence intensity of the red-N-CDs increased with rising pH. The explanation for this behavior is thoroughly discussed in the detection mechanism section (3.7). The impact of varying sodium chloride concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 2.0 mol/L on the fluorescence intensity of both types of carbon dots was also assessed (Fig. S2B). Remarkably, the fluorescence intensity of both blue-N-CDs and red-N-CDs remained almost constant across this range of ionic strengths. This suggests a robust ionic stability in the carbon dots, indicating that their photoluminescent properties are unaffected by changes in the ionic environment, which is advantageous for applications in varying salinity conditions. Furthermore, the durability of these carbon dots under prolonged UV exposure was evaluated over a period of up to 520 min (Fig. S2C). Both blue-N-CDs and red-N-CDs showed no change in fluorescence intensity throughout the exposure duration, confirming excellent photostability. This resistance to photobleaching under continuous UV irradiation demonstrates the potential of these carbon dots for sustained use in applications requiring long-term exposure to light, such as in imaging and sensing technologies.

Several factors influence the amount of ammonia liberated from L-asparaginase upon interaction with L-asparagine, including pH, L-asparagine concentration, temperature, and reaction time. A comprehensive parametric study was conducted to elucidate the influence of various experimental factors on the reaction outcome. The impact of pH on the reaction was studied across a range from 4.0 to 11.0 (Fig. S3A). The highest fluorescence ratio was recorded at pH 7.5, indicating that this is the optimal pH for the interaction between L-asparagine and L-asparaginase. The influence of L-asparagine concentration on the reaction was evaluated across a range from 0.02 to 1.0 mol/L (Fig. S3B). The highest ratio was achieved at L-asparagine concentration of 0.04 mol/L, suggesting that this concentration provides the most favorable conditions for the reaction. The impact of reaction temperature was investigated across a range from 20 to 80 °C (Fig. S3C). The most favorable temperature for the interaction between L-asparagine and L-asparaginase was found to be 37 °C, as this temperature resulted in the highest amount of ammonia liberation. The enzymatic reaction time required for complete degradation of L-asparagine and liberation of the maximum amount of ammonia was evaluated across a range from 1 to 30 min (Fig. S3D). The optimum reaction time was determined to be 10 min. The result of varying incubation time after the addition of both carbon dots was assessed from 0.5 to 10 min (Fig. S3E). It was found that 1 min was sufficient to produce the optimum response, suggesting that the carbon dots rapidly enhance the reaction between L-asparagine and L-asparaginase. Although the excitation wavelength of 350 nm yielded a slightly lower response for the red-N-CDs compared to their optimal excitation wavelength, it was chosen as the excitation wavelength for both carbon dots to ensure a simultaneous response.

To assess the responsiveness of the (blue-N-CDs/red-N-CDs/L-asparagine) system towards L-asparaginase, varying concentrations of L-asparaginase ranging from 20 to 2000 U L-1 were added under optimized conditions. A notable increase in fluorescence at 645 nm of red-N-CDs was observed with escalating concentrations of L-asparaginase, while fluorescence at 445 nm of blue-N-CDs decreased gradually, as shown in Fig. 3 A. Furthermore, the ratio of fluorescence intensities (F645/F445) displayed a linear relationship with L-asparaginase concentration spanning from 20 to 2000 U L-1, depicted in Fig. 3B. This linear dependency is quantified by the regression formula F645/F445 = 0.0012C(L-asparaginase) + 0.11, achieving a correlation coefficient of 0.9981. The established detection limit is 6.95 U L-1, significantly below the effective serum concentration of L-asparaginase used in cancer treatment, which is 750 U L-1 [34]. The innovative system demonstrates considerable potential for analyzing L-asparaginase in biological matrices, surpassing conventional methods such as colorimetry, spectrophotometry, chromatography, and other spectrofluorometric techniques. Table 1 provides a comparative analysis of L-asparaginase detection limits across various methodologies, highlighting the strong performance of the proposed approach for broad-range L-asparaginase sensing. Notably, the proposed method exhibits a lower LOD compared to many other methods. While some methods do show a lower LOD, they are subject to several limitations that were discussed in the introduction section, making this novel approach particularly advantageous.

To investigate the selectivity of the carbon dot-based L-asparaginase detection system (blue-N-CDs/red-N-CDs/L-asparagine) and its resilience against potential biological interferences, fluorescence spectra were monitored in the presence of various interferences at a concentration of 1 mmol/L. These interferences included a broad range of amino acids (such as arginine, cysteine, L-proline, lysine, L-aspartic acid, leucine, glycine, glutamine, histidine, methionine, threonine, tyrosine, ornithine, phenylalanine, and valine), metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Fe2+ and Mn2+), other biological molecules (urea, ascorbic acid, glutathione), and various anions (Cl-, F-, I-, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, S2-, HPO4 2-). According to the data presented in Fig. 4 A and B, no significant alterations in the fluorescence ratio signals were observed in the presence of these potential biological matrix components. This indicates that the detection system exhibits excellent potential for monitoring L-asparaginase activity with minimal interference from complex biological systems. The results affirm the system's high selectivity and capability to function effectively as a probe for L-asparaginase sensing within intricate biological matrices.

The biochemical reaction between L-asparagine and L-asparaginase generates ammonia, consequently raising the pH level and alkalizing the medium. This alteration in pH significantly influences the behavior of the as-prepared carbon dots. The change in pH affects the surface chemistry of these carbon dots through the protonation or deprotonation of their amino and carboxyl functional groups. As the environment becomes more basic, the fluorescence emission intensity of the blue-N-CDs is noticeably reduced, demonstrating a quenching effect on their luminescence. In contrast, the red-N-CDs respond differently; their luminescence intensity increases with the rise in pH, indicating an enhancement in their emission characteristics.

To elucidate the fluorescence quenching mechanism observed in blue-N-CDs under alkaline conditions, comprehensive characterizations were undertaken. These included TEM imaging to assess structural changes at different L-asparaginase levels. The TEM analysis showed that the average particle size of blue-N-CDs remained relatively unchanged (4.2 nm in absence of L-asparaginase and 4.4 nm after addition of L-asparaginase and ammonia liberation) (Fig. 5 A and B). This stability in size across different L-asparaginase concentrations suggests that the fluorescence quenching of blue-N-CDs in alkaline conditions is not due to nanoparticle aggregation. Further investigations involved measuring the zeta (ζ) potentials of the blue-N-CDs at various L-asparaginase levels, revealing a transition from positive to negative as the concentrations was adjusted from 20.0 to 2000.0 U L-1 (Fig. 5C). This change is indicative of the deprotonation of the surface functional groups, which correlates with the pH-induced changes in surface chemistry. Additionally, the ultraviolet visible absorption spectra of the blue-N-CDs were examined in absence and in presence of L-asparaginase (1000.0 U L-1). The results showed an increased absorption in presence of L-asparaginase compared to before addition of L-asparaginase within the wavelength range of 200–600 nm (Fig. S4A). This increase in absorption in presence of L-asparaginase is also ascribed to the deprotonation of surface functional groups.

Elevated pH conditions may promote the deprotonation of surface-bound functional groups on the blue-N-CDs. This process potentially induces alterations in the nanoparticles' surface charge distribution or overall electrostatic properties, which alters their Fermi level and affects their fluorescence [37]. Under acidic conditions, the enhanced formation of hydrogen bonds involving OH groups leads to increased vibrational coupling. This phenomenon contributes to greater structural rigidity of the blue-N-CDs, leading to more intense fluorescence. Conversely, at higher pH levels, the loss of hydrogen bonds due to deprotonation reduces this vibrational coupling, resulting in discrete energy levels and diminished fluorescence [38]. The observed variations in fluorescence intensity of the blue-N-CDs across the pH spectrum can be primarily attributed to reversible protonation and deprotonation processes occurring at their surface functional groups. This pH-dependent behavior is consistent with previous investigations on similar nanostructures [39].

TEM images and zeta potential measurements were utilized to study the behavior of red-N-CDs under varying L-asparaginase levels. At pH 4.0, TEM images displayed well-dispersed carbon dots (2.4 nm). However, in presence of L-asparaginase, the dots tended to aggregate (64.6 nm), likely due to their self-assembly into larger particles (Fig. 5D and E). The red-N-CDs have positively charged surfaces from amino groups, with zeta potential readings drops with increasing L-asparaginase concentrations (Fig. 5F), reflecting the presence of protonated NH3 + groups at low pH levels. This suggests that the transformation from dispersed to aggregated particles under different L-asparaginase concentrations affects their fluorescence, marking the red-N-CDs as potential L-asparaginase-sensitive probes.

To elucidate the interaction between L-asparagine and L-asparaginase, UV–visible spectroscopic analysis was conducted (Fig. S4B). Absorption spectra were acquired for L-asparagine, L-asparaginase, and a mixture of L-asparagine and L-asparaginase. Upon comparison of these spectra, a notable decrease in the absorption intensity of L-asparaginase was observed following the addition of L-asparagine. This spectral change provides compelling evidence for the occurrence of a specific interaction between the L-asparagine and the L-asparaginase. The observed reduction in absorption intensity can be attributed to the enzymatic hydrolysis of L-asparagine by L-asparaginase.

To assess the practical applicability of the (blue-N-CDs/red-N-CDs/L-asparagine) system for detecting l-asparaginase in human serum, an in vitro assay was performed using standard detection protocols. The accuracy of the (blue-N-CDs/red-N-CDs/L-asparagine) probe in quantifying active L-asparaginase concentrations in serum samples was evaluated through recovery rates (%). The results, illustrated in Table 2 , confirm that the recovery rates for the system across three different human serum samples ranged from 96.15 % to 99.75 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 3.13 %. These findings confirm that the (blue-N-CDs/red-N-CDs/L-asparagine) system can reliably and accurately quantify L-asparaginase in human serum, demonstrating its efficacy and precision in a biological matrix.

In conclusion, this study offers a novel fluorometric approach for L-asparaginase sensing using pH-sensitive blue and red nitrogen-doped carbon dots. The approach leverages the pH change induced by ammonia liberation during the L-asparagine-L-asparaginase interaction, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescence response. This method demonstrates high sensitivity, selectivity, and excellent linearity with a low detection limit. Its robust performance in complex biological matrices, as evidenced by successful application in human serum samples, highlights its potential for clinical diagnostics and pharmaceutical quality control. Comprehensive characterization of the carbon dots and elucidation of their response mechanism provide a solid foundation for understanding and optimizing the assay. This simple and cost-effective alternative to traditional L-asparaginase detection methods opens up new possibilities for rapid and accurate enzyme quantification in various fields, potentially improving cancer treatment monitoring and quality control in the pharmaceutical and food industries. Future research could focus on further optimizing the assay for specific applications and exploring similar ratiometric carbon dot systems for detecting other clinically and industrially relevant enzymes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSPD2024R563) at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125161.
